skill.name: azure-cosmos-java
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Cosmos DB Java SDK with synchronous and asynchronous clients, authentication, CRUD operations, queries, partitioning, consistency, request units, and error handling.
skill.summary: Build Java applications that use Azure Cosmos DB NoSQL APIs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static execution and obfuscation findings are false positives caused by markdown code fences, Java method names, tables, and front matter. The skill contains legitimate Azure Cosmos DB examples that read endpoint and key values from environment variables and connect to Azure service URLs, so the residual risk is low and publication is acceptable.

low_findings.title: Credential-based Cosmos DB authentication examples
low_findings.description: The skill shows Java examples that read COSMOS_ENDPOINT and COSMOS_KEY from environment variables and pass the key into CosmosClientBuilder. This is legitimate SDK usage, but users must protect the key and avoid logging or committing it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly reads a Cosmos DB key from the environment and configures SDK authentication. The context is normal cloud database guidance, so the risk is secret-handling exposure rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Azure network endpoints and documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are a placeholder Cosmos DB account endpoint and reference links to Maven, Microsoft Learn, Azure SDK documentation, and Azure samples. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or perform exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and Azure service references in a reference section or placeholder configuration. There is no code that posts credentials to an unrelated endpoint.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misclassified as command execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are caused by triple backticks that delimit XML, bash, and Java examples. They are markdown formatting markers, not Ruby backtick execution or shell commands executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every flagged execution location is inside markdown documentation. The skill file contains no runnable Ruby, shell wrapper, or instruction that executes those fences automatically.

low_findings.title: Static obfuscation and weak-crypto heuristics are unsupported
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged front matter, markdown tables, and the word Cosmos as obfuscation or weak cryptography. No evidence found of encoded payloads, prompt injection, weak crypto configuration, or hidden execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found plain markdown and Java SDK guidance only. The suspicious heuristic labels do not match the semantic content of the referenced lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud credential access through environment variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use System.getenv to load Cosmos DB endpoint and key values for SDK authentication. This is standard practice but requires careful secret management.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Java sample directly accesses named environment variables that may contain a database endpoint and secret key. The behavior is transparent and tied to Azure authentication.

content.user_title: Build Azure Cosmos DB Java Apps
content.value_statement: Java developers need reliable patterns for Cosmos DB clients, containers, queries, and errors. This skill provides concise SDK guidance for synchronous and reactive NoSQL applications.
actual_capabilities: Explains Maven dependency setup for the Azure Cosmos DB Java SDK.
Shows CosmosClient and CosmosAsyncClient authentication patterns.
Guides database and container creation with Java SDK objects.
Covers item create, read, update, delete, and query workflows.
Summarizes partition keys, consistency levels, request units, and 429 handling.
Links to official Azure SDK, Microsoft Learn, samples, and troubleshooting resources.
limitations: Does not provision Azure accounts, databases, containers, or credentials.
Does not validate live Cosmos DB performance or billing impact.
Uses placeholder version values that users must replace with current SDK versions.
Focuses on the NoSQL API and does not cover every Cosmos DB API model.

use_cases.title: Start a Java Cosmos DB integration
use_cases.description: Set up dependencies, client creation, and basic container access for a new Java service.

use_cases.title: Review reactive database workflows
use_cases.description: Use async client examples to plan nonblocking database, container, and item operations.

use_cases.title: Improve production readiness
use_cases.description: Check partition key choices, consistency settings, request unit tracking, and rate-limit handling.

prompt_templates.title: Create a client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure Cosmos DB Java client using environment variables for endpoint and key.

prompt_templates.title: Add CRUD operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design Java Cosmos DB create, read, update, delete, and query operations for a user profile container.

prompt_templates.title: Use async patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my synchronous Cosmos DB Java workflow into an async workflow with CosmosAsyncClient and reactive chaining.

prompt_templates.title: Review production design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Cosmos DB Java design for partition keys, consistency, request unit usage, retries, and regional configuration.

output_examples.input: I need to connect a Java service to Cosmos DB.
output_examples.output: A setup plan covering Maven dependencies, environment variables, client construction, and safe key handling.

output_examples.input: My service needs user profile CRUD operations.
output_examples.output: A Java SDK workflow outline for container access, item creation, reads by partition key, updates, deletes, and queries.

output_examples.input: We see rate limits in production.
output_examples.output: A checklist for request unit monitoring, retry behavior, partition key review, and regional configuration.

best_practices: Use managed identity or a secure secret store when possible instead of long-lived keys.
Choose partition keys with high cardinality and common query access patterns.
Reuse Cosmos DB clients and monitor request units during performance testing.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Cosmos DB keys in source code or prompt history.
Do not create a new CosmosClient for every request.
Do not choose low-cardinality partition keys for high-volume containers.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It explains Java SDK usage, but resource creation still requires Azure access and credentials.

faq.question: Does it support asynchronous Java code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes CosmosAsyncClient patterns and reactive chaining examples.

faq.question: Can it help with partition key design?
faq.answer: Yes. It summarizes key selection criteria and can guide review of container designs.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It shows environment variable usage and should be paired with secure secret management practices.

faq.question: Which Cosmos DB API does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Azure Cosmos DB for NoSQL with the Java SDK.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
